text:
CT CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION:
45-year-old premenopausal Hispanic woman with newly diagnosed left breast mass, biopsy-proven invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3, triple-negative (ER-negative, PR-negative, HER2-negative by IHC and FISH). Ki-67 85%. Fine needle aspiration of a left axillary lymph node has confirmed metastatic carcinoma. Examination requested for systemic staging prior to initiation of neoadjuvant therapy.

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis acquired following administration of 100 mL of iodinated intravenous contrast (Omnipaque 350) in the portal venous phase. Multiplanar reformatted images reviewed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes. Lung windows, soft tissue windows, and bone windows all evaluated.

COMPARISON:
No prior cross-sectional imaging available for comparison. Correlative diagnostic bilateral breast ultrasound and mammography previously performed (reported separately). Diagnostic breast MRI previously performed (reported separately).

--- FINDINGS ---

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The lungs are well-aerated bilaterally. No pulmonary nodule, mass, or consolidation is identified. The major and minor fissures are intact. No ground-glass opacity. No air bronchograms. The central airways are patent. The trachea and main bronchi demonstrate normal caliber without endobronchial lesion.

Pleura: No pleural effusion bilaterally. No pleural thickening or pneumothorax.

Mediastinum: The mediastinal contours are within normal limits for age. No anterior, middle, or posterior mediastinal mass is identified. No pericardial effusion. The heart is normal in size.

Hila: No hilar lymphadenopathy. The hilar fat planes are preserved bilaterally.

Mediastinal and Axillary Lymph Nodes: No pathologically enlarged paratracheal, prevascular, subcarinal, paraesophageal, or internal mammary lymph nodes are identified. In the left axilla, there is a single enlarged lymph node measuring approximately 1.8 cm in short axis with loss of its normal fatty hilum and cortical thickening, morphologically consistent with the known nodal metastasis identified on prior ultrasound and confirmed by FNA cytology. No additional abnormal left axillary nodes are identified at this level of resolution. No right axillary adenopathy. No infraclavicular or supraclavicular adenopathy is visualized on this examination.

Breast/Chest Wall: Given the limited soft tissue contrast of CT relative to dedicated breast imaging, the known left breast primary mass is not separately characterized on this study; dedicated breast MRI has been performed for locoregional staging. No skin thickening or chest wall invasion is identified.

Bones (chest): No lytic or sclerotic osseous lesion is identified in the ribs, clavicles, sternum, scapulae, or visualized thoracic vertebrae. No pathologic fracture.

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size with smooth contour. The hepatic parenchyma is homogeneous and demonstrates uniform enhancement. No focal hepatic lesion suspicious for metastatic disease is identified. No biliary ductal dilatation. The gallbladder is present and unremarkable with no cholelithiasis or wall thickening.

Spleen: Normal in size and attenuation. No focal splenic lesion.

Pancreas: The pancreas enhances uniformly with normal morphology throughout the head, neck, body, and tail. The pancreatic duct is not dilated. No peripancreatic stranding or mass effect.

Adrenal Glands: Both adrenal glands are slender and unremarkable. No adrenal nodule or mass. No evidence of adrenal metastasis.

Kidneys: The kidneys are symmetric in size and position, with brisk and uniform nephrographic enhancement and prompt contrast excretion into the collecting systems bilaterally. No focal renal mass or cortical abnormality. No hydronephrosis. No nephrolithiasis.

Aorta and Major Vessels: The abdominal aorta is normal in caliber with no aneurysmal dilatation or mural thrombus. The celiac axis, superior and inferior mesenteric arteries, and their major branches opacify normally.

Retroperitoneal Lymph Nodes: No retroperitoneal, para-aortic, aortocaval, or common iliac lymphadenopathy is identified. Lymph nodes visualized in the retroperitoneum are small and within normal limits.

Mesentery and Peritoneum: No mesenteric nodularity, omental infiltration, or peritoneal implants are identified. The mesentery and omentum appear normal. No ascites.

Bowel: Visualized loops of small and large bowel are unremarkable in caliber and wall thickness. No bowel obstruction or wall thickening. No free intraperitoneal air.

PELVIS:

Uterus and Ovaries: The uterus is anteverted and normal in size, with no intracavitary or intramural mass identified. The endometrium is thin. Both ovaries are visible and appear normal without suspicious lesions, mass, or cystic complexity.

Pelvic Lymph Nodes: No pelvic sidewall, external iliac, internal iliac, or obturator lymphadenopathy. No inguinal lymphadenopathy.

Bladder: Normal-appearing urinary bladder with no wall thickening or intraluminal mass.

Other: No free fluid in the pelvis. No peritoneal lesions.

BONE WINDOWS (entire examination):

The visualized osseous structures are surveyed on bone windows, including the thoracic and lumbar spine, pelvis, sacrum, proximal femora, and ribs. No lytic lesion, sclerotic lesion, or cortical destruction is identified. The vertebral body heights are maintained. No evidence of osseous metastatic involvement.

--- IMPRESSION ---

No pulmonary nodules, pleural effusion, mediastinal lymphadenopathy, or evidence of intrathoracic metastatic disease.

In the left axilla, a single morphologically abnormal lymph node (approximately 1.8 cm, effaced hilum) is present, consistent with the known nodal metastasis. No additional axillary, supraclavicular, or infraclavicular lymphadenopathy is identified.

The liver, spleen, pancreas, adrenal glands, and kidneys are unremarkable, with no hepatic or visceral lesions suspicious for metastatic involvement.

No retroperitoneal, mesenteric, or pelvic lymphadenopathy.

No peritoneal or omental deposits. No ascites.

No osseous lesions to suggest skeletal metastatic disease on bone windows throughout the axial and proximal appendicular skeleton.

Overall, imaging of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis does not demonstrate evidence of distant metastatic disease. In conjunction with the known left breast primary and ipsilateral axillary nodal involvement, findings support locoregional disease confined to the left breast and left axilla (cT2N1M0), consistent with Stage IIB breast cancer by clinical staging criteria.
